title: Jake Johnson and Joe Swanberg on Their New Film ‘Win It All’ | The Big Picture (Ep. 8)
format: Chris Ryan and Sean Fennessey sit down at South by Southwest with actor Jake Johnson and director Joe Swanberg to talk about Win It All, the self-financed poker movie they wrote together and sold to Netflix. The conversation runs through their three films together, Swanberg's mumblecore roots, and why they'd rather put the thing straight in front of people at home than tour it city to city.

segments.heading: Jake Johnson and Joe Swanberg at SXSW
segments.guest: Jake Johnson and Joe Swanberg on

films.title: Win It All
films.note: The film they're there for: Johnson plays Eddie Garrett, a small-time Chicago gambler who's handed a bag to hold and can't leave it alone. Johnson and Swanberg co-wrote it and put in their own money, then took only it to Netflix. Johnson pulled the details of Eddie's look (the gold rope chain, the hair) from years of sitting next to that guy at the table. The casino sets were built from scratch by production designer Amy Holmberg, and the poker was shot with 50-plus real gamblers playing for chips so the hands weren't faked.

films.title: Drinking Buddies
films.note: The first Johnson-Swanberg collaboration, made while Johnson was between seasons of New Girl. Swanberg's pitch (short days, improv, an IPA or two) is what got him in, and Johnson credits his story notes with helping the film reach a far bigger audience than Swanberg's earlier work, largely on Netflix.

films.title: Digging for Fire
films.note: Their second film together, shot in Los Angeles and the first they fully self-financed. It made its money back plus a little, which is why Win It All felt like house money.

films.title: Happy Christmas
films.note: Swanberg cites it, right after Drinking Buddies, as part of learning that the people who bring the most value to a movie should be the ones benefiting most from it.

films.title: Hannah Takes the Stairs
films.note: Swanberg's third feature and his first real burst of attention on the small indie scene, before a run of films almost nobody saw.

films.title: Alexander the Last
films.note: Produced by Noah Baumbach; another early Swanberg film that drew some notice during his ups-and-downs period on the indie circuit.

films.title: Safety Not Guaranteed
films.note: Swanberg acted in it, and points to feeling the audience lean forward once its story engine kicked in: the sensation he and Johnson chased on Win It All.

films.title: California Split
films.note: Swanberg's favorite of the gambling movies and, he says, a perfect film: the reference point he'd most welcome, so long as it lands in essence rather than as a copied shot. He deliberately avoids rewatching the classics before writing so they don't leak in.

films.title: Rounders
films.note: Named as one of the gambling movies Sean rattled off leaving the theater; the kind of thing Johnson will stop on whenever it's on cable.

films.title: The Gambler
films.note: The original, invoked alongside California Split and Rounders as a touchstone for Win It All.

films.title: The Cincinnati Kid
films.note: Another gambling-movie reference point that came up as the hosts placed Win It All in the canon.

films.title: Apocalypse Now
films.note: Johnson brings up Coppola putting up every asset he had as the ultimate version of Swanberg gambling his own money on their films.

films.title: Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse
films.note: The making-of documentary about Apocalypse Now, shot by Coppola's wife; Johnson asks whether Swanberg has seen it while making the gambling analogy.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Johnson had just seen it and calls it perfect: a funny film with a monster of a story engine, the model he and Swanberg want to push toward next.

films.title: The Mummy
films.note: Johnson's big studio job opposite Tom Cruise, which he sets against the Swanberg films: a great gig with no gamble in it.

films.title: Die Hard
films.note: His shorthand for rewatchability (the movie you catch 30 times over a summer), which he thinks Win It All is built for.

films.title: Waterworld
films.note: Turns up in Johnson's riff about actors who quietly bankroll their own dream projects and end up broke.

Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocal.title: Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse
